coredata.dc:title: Orange Luminescent Carbon Dots as Fluorescent Probe for Detection of Nitrite
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: JIA, Jing
LU, Wen-Jing
LI, Lin
JIAO, Yuan
GAO, Yi-Fang
SHUANG, Shao-Min
coredata.dc:description: Orange luminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment with p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. The morphology and optical properties of the N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV-vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs had an extreme small size ((1.62 ± 0.35) nm), good water solubility and excellent fluorescent stability. The addition of nitrite (NO2 −) caused fluorescence increase of N-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new fluorescence analysis method for detecting NO2 − was established. Moreover, the possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism was proposed. The constructed fluorescence sensing system was applied to the detection of NO2 − in sausage, pickle and tap water samples with recoveries from 97.1% to 104.5% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.1% to 3.5%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange fluorescence
Enhanced fluorescent probe
Nitrite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 47, Issue 4, April 2019 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 ________________________ Received 19 December 2018; accepted 24 January 2019 *Corresponding author. Email: smshuang@sxu.edu.cn This work was supported by the Shanxi Returned Overseas Students Foundation of China (No. 2017-key1) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575084, 21874087). Copyright Â© 2019, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(19)61155-2 RESEARCH PAPER Orange Luminescent Carbon Dots as Fluorescent Probe for Detection of Nitrite JIA Jing1, LU Wen-Jing1, LI Lin1, JIAO Yuan2, GAO Yi-Fang2, SHUANG Shao-Min1,* 1 School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China 2 Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China Abstract: Orange luminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment with p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. The morphology and optical properties of the N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV-vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs had an extreme small size ((1.62 Â± 0.35) nm), good water solubility and excellent fluorescent stability. The addition of nitrite (NO2â€’) caused fluorescence increase of N-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new fluorescence analysis method for detecting NO2â€’ was established. Moreover, the possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism was proposed. The constructed fluorescence sensing system was applied to the detection of NO2â€’ in sausage, pickle and tap water samples with recoveries from 97.1% to 104.5% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.1% to 3.5%. Key Words: Carbon dots; Orange fluorescence; Enhanced fluorescent probe; Nitrite 1 Introduction Nitrite is widely used in food processing as an additive or preservative, due to its ability to inhibit the growth of bacterial spoilage, colour the cured meat and enhance flavor[1]. Nitrite can react with amine foods and convert to toxic N- nitrosamines, which may lead to malformations and cancer[2]. Besides, excessive nitrite will combine with hemoglobin in human blood to form methemoglobin, which reduces blood oxygen transportation capacity and causes tissue hypoxia[3]. Currently, major detection methods of nitrite involve spectrophotometry[4], electrochemistry[5], chemiluminescence[6] and chromatography[7]. Among them, fluorometry has attracted wide interest because of its simplicity and sensitivity. Many fluorescent probes have been developed to detect nitrite, such as small molecule probes[8], semiconductor quantum dots[9], metal nanoclusters[10]. However, these probes usually require specialized synthesis techniques and complicated purification procedures, or probes themselves have poor water solubility, high toxicity, and long detection time, which limit their further application in environmental and biology related fields to varying degrees. Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple, sensitive, and biocompatible fluorescent probes for detection of NO2â€’. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have been used in optical sensing[11], bioimaging[12], medical diagnosis[13] etc, owing to their excellent optical properties, easy surface functionali- zation, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. In particular, fluorescent nano-sensing methods based on CDs have been used to detect metal ions[14,15], anions[16] and biomolecules[17,18]. However, few studies were involved in the detection of NO2â€’ based on CDs. Zhang et al[19] synthesized blue fluorescent N-CNDs for NO2â€’ determination based on fluorescence quenching behavior. From then on, some other CDs that could be used for direct and rapid detection of nitrite were successively reported[20,21]. Unfortunately, these CDs just exhibited blue/green emission, which limited their further JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 applications, particularly in the biomedical fields because of the blue auto-fluorescence of the biological matrix and photodamage of biological tissues under UV excitation light[22]. Xiang et al[1] attempted to extend the detection wavelength of nitrite to long wavelength region. They grafted CDs and rhodamine B onto silica nanoparticles to construct a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of nitrite in the yellow luminescence region. It is worth noting that those above studies were based on fluorescence quenching to detect nitrite. Compared with quenching probes, the enhanced fluorescent probes have many advantages, such as relatively low interferences and detection limits, which can reduce the generation of false positive signals and the interference of dark background[23]. One of attractive strategies for NO2â€’ detection was reported very recently, in which CDs were used as a donor and neutral red as a receptor to construct a colorimetric fluorescent dual-mode sensing system for NO2â€’ detection based on the enhancement effect of fluorescence[24]. However, studies on detection of NO2â€’ based on the fluorescence enhancement effect have rarely been reported. Therefore, development of enhanced fluorescent probes with long-wavelength emission for detection of NO2â€’ is still a challenge. Herein, we used p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors to prepare orange emissive CDs by one-step hydrothermal. The prepared N-CDs exhibited extremely small size ((1.62 Â± 0.35) nm), excellent photostability and good water solubility. Importantly, the addition of NO2â€’ enhanced fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. On the basis of this phenomenon, a new method for detecting NO2â€’ was constructed. The linear range of NO2- was 8â€“100 Î¼M with a detection limit of 0.65 Î¼M. Meanwhile, the obtained N-CDs were further applied to detection of NO2- in sausage, pickle and tap water samples, showing good practicability. 2 Experimental 2.1 Instrumentations and reagents Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out on a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL Ltd., Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained on a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany) with the KBr pellet technique. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of dried N-CDs powder were acquired on an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan). The UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired on UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. Elemental analysis of N-CDs was carried out by using the vario EL CUBE elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany). A FE20 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) was used to adjust pH values. Zeta potentials of the N-CDs in different pH solutions were measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK). p-Phenylenediamine was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and sodium nitrite were purchased from Beijing Red Star Chemical Plant. Rhodamine B, potassium ferrocyanide, zinc acetate and borax were provided by Tianjin Jinbei Fine Chemical. Deionized water (â‰¥ 18.25 MÎ© cm) from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore) was used in all experiments. 2.2 Synthesis of N-CDs The N-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment using p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as raw materials. Briefly, 0.42 g of citric acid and 0.16 g of p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water and stirred for 2 min to form a homogeneous mixture. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted at 180 Â°C for 4 h. The obtained N-CDs solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles or precipitates. Subsequently, the N-CDs aqueous solution was further purified by continuous dialysis (MWCO of 500 Da) for 24 h to remove excess other small molecules. Finally, the solution was freeze dried to obtain solid. 2.3 Measurement of quantum yield Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs was determined by using Rhodamine B (QY = 0.89) in ethanol as reference based on the following equation: Î¦ = Î¦R(n/nR)(AR/A)(Î·/Î·R)2 (1) where, Î¦ represents the quantum yield, n refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity, A represents the absorbance, and Î· is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R stands for reference to Rhodamine B. To prevent the re-absorption effect, the absorbances of the N-CDs and Rhodamine B solutions were kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength of 520 nm. 2.4 Selectivity and fluorescence detection of nitrite Different concentrations of NO2â€’ (10 Î¼L) were respectively added into 1 mL of N-CDs solution (0.30 mg mLâ€’1). After standing for 3 min, the emission spectra of resulting sample solutions were measured. The selectivity of N-CDs toward NO2â€’ was investigated by adding 10 Î¼L of other anions (10 mM) instead of NO2â€’. 2.5 Detection of actual samples Sausage and bagged pickles were purchased from local supermarkets, and the water samples were laboratory tap JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 water. Approximately 20 g of sausage was minced and placed in a 500-mL beaker. Then 12.5 mL of saturated borax solution and 300 mL of deionized water at 70 Â°C were added. The mixture was placed in a boiling water bath for 15 min. After cooling down to room temperature, it was transferred to a 500-mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, 5 mL of potassium ferrocyanide and 5 mL of zinc acetate solutions were added to precipitate protein. Finally, the volume was adjusted to 500 mL followed by filtration through filter papers, and the resulting filtrate was stored at 4 Â°C. The pickles were homogenized before the measurement. Then 20 g of the sample was added into a 500-mL beaker. The pickles samples were processed by the same way as the sausage samples described above. Tap water was boiled for 30 min before use, and then filtrated through a 0.22-Î¼m microporous membrane. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Characterization of N-CDs The morphology and size distribution of N-CDs were investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig.1A, N-CDs exhibit a relatively regular spherical shape and are uniformly distributed. The average particle diameter was about (1.62 Â± 0.35) nm (inset Fig.1A). The analyses of elemental composition and surface functional groups of N-CDs were performed by the elemental analysis, FTIR spectroscopy, and XPS characterizations. Elemental analysis indicates that the obtained N-CDs mainly consist of C 48.03% (w/w), H 4.89% (w/w), N 7.79% (w/w) and O (calculated value) 39.29% (w/w). The FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig.1B. The absorption peak at 3449 cm-1 arouses from stretching vibrations of Oâ€’H or Nâ€’H. The weak band at 2980 and 2932 cm-1 are caused by Câ€’H. The peak at 1712 cmâ€’1 indicates the presence of C=O. The absorption peaks at 1516 and 1400 cmâ€’1 correspond to Nâ€’H and Câ€’N bond, respectively. The presence of the Câ€’O bond results in an absorption peak at 1209 cmâ€’1. The full range XPS (Fig.1C) spectra exhibit three peaks at 283.7, 398.2 and 530.1 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig.1D) indicates the presence of C=C (284.7 eV), Câ€’N (285.9 eV), C=O (288.5 eV). As shown in Fig.1E, O1s could be divided into two peaks, 531.8 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to C=O and Câ€’OH/Câ€’Oâ€’C, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig.1F) shows two peaks centered at 400.0 and 401.2 eV, which are attributed to the Câ€’Nâ€’C and O=Câ€’N. The surface composition of the N-CDs identified by XPS was consistent with the FTIR results. Based on the above results, we speculated that the N-CDs contained carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide and amino groups. 3.2 Photoluminescence properties of N-CDs Figure 2A shows UV-vis absorption spectrum, optimal fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of N-CDs. As shown in Fig.2A, N-CDs has a significant absorption peak at 520 nm, which might be attributed to nâ†’ Ï€* transition of the surface functional groups of N-CDs. When excited at 520 nm, the N-CDs show maximum PL at around 600 nm. The aqueous solution of N-CDs showed orange luminescence under UV light (365 nm). In addition, the stability of the as-prepared CDs under different conditions was investigated. As shown in Fig.2B, the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs solution hardly changes in the range of pH 3â€’12, indicating that they are less disturbed by pH. The effect of concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence Fig.1 (A) TEM image, (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) XPS spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (D) C1s, (E) O1s, and (F) N1s of N-CDs. Inset in A is size distribution histograms of the synthesized N-CDs JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 Fig.2 (A) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. Effects of (B) pH, (C) ionic strength and (D) irradiation time with xenon arc light on fluorescence intensity of N-CDs intensity of N-CDs was examined (Fig.2C). It is observed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs does not change much even at ion concentration up to 1 M. In addition, N-CDs still show excellent fluorescence stability after continuous illumination with a 500 W Xenon lamp for 120 min (Fig.2D). Using Rhodamine B as a reference, QY of the synthesized N-CDs at 520 nm excitation wavelength was calculated to be 4.8%. 3.3 Detection of nitrite based on N-CDs The effect of 18 kinds of different anions on fluorescence of N-CDs was examined for assessing the selectivity toward NO2â€’. As shown in Fig.3A, only NO2â€’ bring significantly enhancement impact on the fluorescence intensity, whereas other metal ions are negligible or have no influence. This revealed that the as-synthesized N-CDs exhibited higher selectivity to NO2â€’. The response time of N-CDs toward NO2- was further studied. Approximately 10 Î¼L (10 mM) of NO2- was added to the N-CDs solution (0.30 mg mLâ€’1). With extension of the mixing time, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually increases and tended to be stable after 3 min (Fig.3B). As shown in Fig.3C, fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs gradually increases as the concentration of NO2â€’ increases from 0 Î¼M to 100 Î¼M. Insets in Fig. 3C show the fluorescence emission of the N-CDs before and after the addition of NO2â€’ under UV light of 365 nm. It is apparent that the orange emission is enhanced after adding NO2â€’ to the N-CDs solution. Figure 3D displays a good linear relationship between the enhancement efficiency (F â€’ F0)/F0 and NO2â€’ concentration in the range of 8â€“100 Î¼M (R2 = 0.997) with a detection limit of 0.65 Î¼M (S/N = 3). In addition, the detection performance of NO2â€’ by different methods was compared (Table 1). The obtained N-CDs had a fast response time towards NO2â€’ and a relatively low detection limit of 0.65 Î¼M, which was dramatically lower than the maximum permitted value of 65 Î¼M for NO2â€’ in drinking water specified by the World Health Organization. Compared to quenching fluorescent probes in Table 1, the obtained N-CDs, as fluorescent-enhanced probes, could reduce the generation of false positive signals and the interference of dark background. In addition, the N-CDs could detect NO2â€’ in the long wavelength region (Î»Em = 600 nm), while other fluorescent probes were limited in detection in the blue-green luminescent region. 3.4 Possible mechanism of optical response of N-CDs to NO2â€’ The UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, NO2â€’, N-CDs + NO2â€’ (the physical addition spectra and experimental spectrum) are shown in Fig.4A. It is shown that the experimental curve dose not coincide with the theoretical curve, indicating a chemical reaction takes place between N-CDs and NO2â€’ to form new complex. To further confirm this deduction, FT-IR, zeta-potential, and hydrated particle size of the N-CDs were JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 Fig.3 (A) Selectivity of N-CDs towards NO2â€’, (B) Response time of N-CDs towards NO2â€’, (C) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of NO2â€’, and (D) Linear relationship between (F â€’ F0)/F0 and NO2â€’ concentration Table 1 Comparison of performance of different methods for detection of nitrite Materials Detection Method Detection wavelength (nm) Response time (min) Linear Range (Î¼M) LOD (Î¼M) Ref â€’ Chromatography â€’ 3 2.89 Ã— 103â€’2.89 Ã— 105 6.25 [7] Carbon nanofibers Electrochemistry â€’ â€’ 5â€’300 3 [2] Paper-based analytical devices Colorimetry â€’ 15 0â€’100 11.3 [25] Carbon dots Chemiluminescence â€’ â€’ 0.1~10 0.053 [6] Copper nanoclusters Fluorometry (quenching) 422 3 10â€’225 3.4 [10] CNDs Fluorometry (quenching) 390 â€’ 15â€’1110 13.5 [26] N-CDs Fluorometry (enhancement) 600 3 8â€’100 0.65 This work â€’, no materials or no detection wavelength or not mentioned response time. investigated. In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs + NO2â€’ complex (Fig.4B), a new peak at 1633 cmâ€’1 is found. Compared with the slight absorption of NO2â€’ at 1633 cmâ€’1, the absorption peak of N-CDs + NO2â€’ is significantly enhanced, which might be due to NO2â€’ interacting with the nitrogen- containing functional groups of the N-CDs. After addition of NO2â€’, the zeta potential value changed from (5.69 Â± 0.09) mV to (â€’1.77 Â± 0.06) mV. It indicated that NO2- was selectively coordinated with the amino group of the N-CDs, and the positive charge was shielded. In addition, the hydrated particle sizes of N-CDs before and after the addition of NO2â€’ were examined. As shown in Fig.4D, the average hydrated particle size of N-CDs + NO2â€’ is (446 Â± 35) nm, which is much higher than the average particle size of N-CDs ((33 Â± 7) nm, Fig.4C). This indicates that aggregation occurrs between N-CDs and NO2â€’, which induces fluorescence enhancement. 3.5 Detection of NO2â€’ in actual samples Commercially available sausages, bagged pickles, and laboratory tap water samples were processed as described in Section 2.5, and the detection results are shown in Table 2. The recoveries of those samples ranged from 97.1% to 104.5% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.1% and 3.5%. These results further confirmed the reliability and feasibility of the N-CDs-based nanosensors for monitoring NO2â€’ in actual samples. 4 Conclusions In summary, orange emissive N-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CDs have extremely small size, good water solubility and excellent photostability. Based on fluorescence enhancement, a new NO2â€’ detection method was established. This method had high sensitivity and good selectivity for NO2â€’. Meanwhile, the synthesized N-CDs were further applied to the detection of JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 Fig.4 (A) UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CDs (0.30 mg mLâ€’1), NO2â€’ (100 Î¼M), N-CDs + NO2â€’ (the theoretical curve) and N-CDs + NO2â€’ (the experimental curve), (B) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, NO2â€’ and N-CDs + NO2â€’. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements of (C) N-CDs and (D) N-CDs + NO2â€’ Table 2 Detection result of nitrite in sausage, pickle and tap water samples Sample Detected (mg kg-1) Added (mg kgâ€’1) Found (mg kgâ€’1) Recovery (%, n = 6) RSD (%, n = 6) Sausage 16.4 34.9 52.6 103.7 3.2 69.7 87.0 101.3 3.3 104.6 118.9 98.0 2.8 Pickle 66.7 34.9 100.6 97.1 2.8 69.7 135.0 98.0 1.4 104.6 172.7 101.3 3.5 Tap water* 0 20.0 20.9 104.5 1.1 40.0 39.0 97.5 2.5 60.0 61.7 102.8 2.0 * The unit of concentration is Î¼M. NO2â€’ in sausage, pickle and tap water samples with satisfactory results. The synthesized N-CDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe have great potentials in the field of food safety monitoring. References [1] Xiang G Q, Wang Y L, Zhang H, Fan H H, Fan L, He L J, Jiang X M, Zhao W J. Food Chem., 2018, 260: 13â€“18 [2] Li L B, Liu D, Wang K, Mao H P, You T Y. Sens. ACS Appl. JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 Mater. Interfaces, 2016, 8: 23533â€“23541 [14] Gu D, Shang S M, Yu Q, Shen J. Appl. Surf. Acta, 2017, 968: 74â€“84 [21] Feng Z B, Li Z L, Zhang X W, Shi Y P, Zhou N. Molecules, 2017, 22(12): 2061 [22] Gao Y F, Jiao Y, Lu W J, Liu Y, Han H, Gong X J, Xian M, Shuang S M, Dong C. J. Mater. Chem. B, 2018, 6: 6099â€“6107 [23] Purbia R, Paria S. Biosens. Bioelectron., 2016, 79: 467â€“475 [24] Hua X T, Shi J Y, Shi Y Q, Zou X B, Tahira H E, Holmes M, Zhang W, Huang X W, Li Z H, Xu Y W. Meat Sci., 2019, 147: 127â€“134 [25] Cardoso T M G, Garcia P T, Coltro W K T. Anal. Methods, 2015, 7: 7311 [26] Karalia K K, Sygellou L, Stalikasa C D. Talanta, 2018, 189: 480â€“488 ere synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CDs have extremely small size, good water solubility and excellent photostability. Based on fluorescence enhancement, a new NO2â€’ detection method was established. This method had high sensitivity and good selectivity for NO2â€’. Meanwhile, the synthesized N-CDs were further applied to the detection of JIA Jing et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2019, 47(4): 560â€“566 Fig.4 (A) UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CDs (0.30 mg mLâ€’1), NO2â€’ (100 Î¼M), N-CDs + NO2â€’ (the theoretical curve) and N-CDs + NO2â€’ (the experimental curve), (B) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, NO2â€’ and N-CDs + NO2â€’. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements of (C) N-CDs and (D) N-CDs + NO2â€’ Table 2 Detection result of nitrite in sausage, pickle and tap water samples Sample Detected (mg kg-1) Added (mg kgâ€’1) Found (mg kgâ€’1) Recovery (%, n = 6) RSD (%, n = 6) Sausage 16.4 34.9 52.6 103.7 3.2 69.7 87.0 101.3 3.3 104.6 118.

head.ce:title: Orange Luminescent Carbon Dots as Fluorescent Probe for Detection of Nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Orange luminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment with p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. The morphology and optical properties of the N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV-vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs had an extreme small size ((1.62 ± 0.35) nm), good water solubility and excellent fluorescent stability. The addition of nitrite (NO2 −) caused fluorescence increase of N-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new fluorescence analysis method for detecting NO2 − was established. Moreover, the possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism was proposed. The constructed fluorescence sensing system was applied to the detection of NO2 − in sausage, pickle and tap water samples with recoveries from 97.1% to 104.5% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.1% to 3.5%.

Orange luminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment by using p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. The addition of nitrite (NO2 −) caused a fluorescence increase of N-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new fluorescence analysis method for detecting NO2 − was established.
